questions.question: What is an enumeration in Java and what are its advantages over traditional int constants?
keyPoints: Definition: Enum is a special data type defining fixed set of constants
Type safety: Compiler prevents invalid values
Compile-time checking: Errors caught before runtime
Better semantics: Meaningful names vs magic numbers
Can have methods, constructors, implement interfaces
Provides OOP features

questions.question: Explain the java.lang.Enum class and its important methods.
keyPoints: All enums implicitly extend java.lang.Enum
name() - returns constant name as String
ordinal() - returns position (0-based)
values() - returns array of all constants
valueOf(String name) - returns constant by name
compareTo() - compares based on ordinal
toString() - returns name by default

questions.question: How can enums have constructors and fields in Java? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Enums can have constructors (always private)
Constructor is called once for each constant
Fields can be instance or static
Each constant can pass arguments to constructor
Example: enum Color { RED(255,0,0), GREEN(0,255,0); private int r,g,b; Color(int r,int g,int b){...}}

questions.question: Can enums implement interfaces? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Yes, enums can implement any number of interfaces
Useful for defining behavior
Each constant inherits interface methods
Example: enum Operation implements Calculable { PLUS{public double calc(double a,double b){return a+b;}}, MINUS{...}}

questions.question: What are abstract methods in enums? How are they implemented?
keyPoints: Enums can declare abstract methods
Each enum constant must provide implementation
Used when different constants need different behavior
All constants act as anonymous inner classes implementing the abstract method
Example: enum Priority { HIGH{void perform(){}}, MEDIUM{void perform(){}}, LOW{void perform(){}}; abstract void perform();}

questions.question: Explain the use of enums in switch statements and its benefits.
keyPoints: Enums can be used directly in switch without enum name
Compiler ensures all cases are covered (if using default optional)
Type safety at compile time
No need for fall-through (can omit break)
More readable than int-based switch
Better IDE support with auto-completion

questions.question: Design an enum called 'Level' with three constants: LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH. Each constant should have an associated priority value (1, 2, 3). Write a Java program that demonstrates: (i) Defining the enum with constructor and fields (ii) Using values() and valueOf() methods (iii) Using ordinal() method (iv) Using enum in switch statement.
keyPoints: Define enum Level with constructor accepting int priority
Define getPriority() method
Demonstrate values() - returns all constants
Demonstrate valueOf() - convert string to enum
Demonstrate ordinal() - get position
Demonstrate switch with enum constants
Complete working code with main method
Output demonstration

questions.question: Create an enum 'Operation' that implements an interface 'Calculate'. The interface has a method 'apply(double a, double b)'. The enum should have constants PLUS, MINUS, MULTIPLY, DIVIDE with appropriate implementations. Write a program to demonstrate polymorphism using enums.
keyPoints: Define interface Calculate with abstract method apply()
Define enum Operation implementing Calculate
Implement apply() differently for each constant
Demonstrate polymorphism - can store enum in interface reference
Show switch-case usage
Show valueOf() to get operation from string
Complete program with test cases

questions.question: (a) Explain why enums provide better type safety than int constants. (b) Can an enum extend another enum? Justify your answer. (c) What is the purpose of the 'name()' method in enums and how does it differ from 'toString()'?
keyPoints: (a) Type safety: Compiler catches type mismatches, no invalid values like with int constants, self-documenting code
(b) No, enums cannot extend another enum - already extend java.lang.Enum, no multiple inheritance in Java, compile-time constant set
(c) name() returns exact constant name as declared, final method, cannot be overridden. toString() by default returns name but can be overridden to provide custom display.

questions.question: Explain the internal implementation of enums in Java. How does the compiler translate enums? Discuss singleton property of enum constants and how this is used in design patterns.
keyPoints: Compiler creates a class extending java.lang.Enum
Each constant becomes a public static final field (singleton instances)
values() method is compiler-generated synthetic method
Constructor arguments become parameters
For singleton: enum can only have one instance per constant, thread-safe by JVM, serialization guaranteed
Design patterns: Enum singleton pattern is recommended over classical singleton
